lists.title: Spanish A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in Plan Curricular (Instituto Cervantes) order: pronouns and nouns first, then articles, the two copulas, present-tense verb families, negation, existence, and questions.

points.title: Subject pronouns (yo, tú, él…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: The subject pronouns are: yo (I), tú (you, informal), él/ella (he/she), usted (you, formal), nosotros/nosotras (we), vosotros/vosotras (you all, Spain), ellos/ellas (they), ustedes (you all). Because the verb ending already shows the person, Spanish usually DROPS the pronoun — you say it mainly for emphasis or contrast: 'Yo soy de México (pero él no)'.
points.culture_note: tú vs usted matters socially: tú with friends, family, and peers; usted with strangers, elders, and formal situations. Regions differ — much of Latin America uses ustedes for ALL plural 'you'.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation: Every Spanish noun is masculine or feminine. Most nouns in -o are masculine (libro) and most in -a are feminine (casa). Plurals add -s after a vowel (casa → casas) and -es after a consonant (ciudad → ciudades). Gender and number ripple through the whole sentence — articles and adjectives must match the noun.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Definite articles (el / la)
points.explanation: Spanish nouns have grammatical gender. Use 'el' before masculine singular nouns and 'la' before feminine singular nouns; the plurals are 'los' and 'las'. Most nouns ending in -o are masculine (el libro) and most ending in -a are feminine (la casa), but there are exceptions you learn word by word.
points.culture_note: Gender is grammatical, not literal — 'el mapa' (the map) is masculine despite ending in -a, a Greek-derived exception.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Indefinite articles (un / una)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: 'Un' goes before masculine singular nouns and 'una' before feminine singular nouns: un perro, una casa. The plurals 'unos/unas' mean 'some'. Use the indefinite article to introduce something new; after that, switch to el/la because it is now known.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: 'Ser' expresses permanent or defining qualities: identity, origin, profession. Present forms: soy, eres, es, somos, sois, son. Use ser for who/what something is (Soy estudiante), and the separate verb estar for temporary states and location.
points.culture_note: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be' — ser (essence) and estar (state). 'Es aburrido' = he is boring; 'está aburrido' = he is bored.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — ser and estar

points.title: The verb estar — location and states
points.function: Say where things are and how you feel
points.explanation: 'Estar' covers location and temporary states: estoy, estás, está, estamos, estáis, están. Location ALWAYS takes estar, even for permanent places: 'Madrid está en España'. States that can change — tired, cold, happy — also take estar: 'Estoy cansado'.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
points.explanation: Regular -ar verbs (hablar, estudiar, trabajar) drop -ar and add endings: -o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an. So hablar → hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, habláis, hablan.
points.culture_note: -ar verbs are the largest and most regular Spanish verb group, so mastering this pattern unlocks hundreds of verbs at once.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Present tense of -er and -ir verbs
points.function: Talk about eating, drinking, living, and more
points.explanation: -er verbs (comer, beber) take -o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en; -ir verbs (vivir, escribir) are the same except nosotros/vosotros: -imos, -ís. So como, comes, come, comemos… and vivo, vives, vive, vivimos…

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Negation with no
points.explanation: Put 'no' directly before the conjugated verb: 'No tengo tiempo' (I don't have time), 'Ella no habla inglés'. Answering a question negatively often doubles it: '¿Hablas francés? — No, no hablo francés.'

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

points.title: Hay — there is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: 'Hay' (from haber) is invariable: it means both 'there is' and 'there are'. 'Hay un libro en la mesa', 'Hay tres cafés', 'No hay leche'. Use hay for existence; use estar to locate something specific you already know about.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

drills.translation: There is a book on the table.

points.title: Question words (qué, dónde, cómo…)
points.function: Ask about things, places, people, and time
points.explanation: The interrogatives carry a written accent: qué (what), dónde (where), cómo (how), quién (who), cuándo (when), cuánto (how much). Questions are wrapped in ¿…?: '¿Dónde estás?', '¿Cómo te llamas?'. Word order stays simple — question word + verb.
points.culture_note: The upside-down ¿ opens every question in writing — it tells the reader the intonation before the sentence starts.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2

drills.translation: What is your name? (lit. how do you call yourself)

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.explanation: Adjectives match their noun in gender and number: casa blanca, libros nuevos. Adjectives in -o have four forms (blanco/blanca/blancos/blancas); adjectives in -e or a consonant only change for number (grande/grandes, azul/azules). Most adjectives FOLLOW the noun.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
